personality.voice: soft, careful, more composed than the situation warrants
personality.disposition: She asks for very little but she notices everything. She has made herself invisible to the town through long practice — people look past her the way they look past furniture, which is a thing she worked out is useful. She has watched Blue Lantern Alley for three years and could write a report that would astonish the guard. She is waiting for the right person to give it to.
personality.backstory: Her name was Petra Marsh before she was the Widow Marsh, and before that it was Petra Sowell, and she had a house and a husband and savings in a box under a board. She lost all three in a sequence of events she doesn't describe in detail because she has found that telling it in partial requires less of her. She has been on the steps at Blue Lantern Alley for three years. She is not broken — she knows what broken would feel like and this is not it. She is reduced, and she knows exactly how reduced she is, and what it would take to be otherwise, and she is working toward it with the patience she has in place of resources. Brother Aldwin brings bread. She gives him warnings when she sees things he should know about. She considers this a fair arrangement.
motivations: Get through this with her dignity intact — she considers dignity distinct from comfort and defends the former
Tell someone the right piece of information at the right moment
A room of her own, eventually — she has a number in her head and she is counting toward it
fears: Being moved along to somewhere without even the cover of the alley steps
The men who use the back of Hodge's shop noticing that she sees them — she has been careful but not invisible enough perhaps
mannerisms: Eyes track the alley constantly with a calm that looks like vacancy
Accepts things with composed gratitude — not performance, but genuine without being servile
speechPatterns: Speaks quietly and waits to see if you will lean in — she is screening for people who will listen
Offers information with economy — not everything at once, not overly transactional
Uses descriptions rather than names: 'the man with the gray cart', 'the woman who came twice last week'
Never presses; if you don't engage she simply waits for the next person who might

secretsHeld: Has observed Hodge's complete operation for three years — faces, schedules, volumes, anomalies
Three weeks ago she heard a conversation through the alley wall that she was absolutely not meant to hear, involving a name she doesn't recognize and an object she can describe precisely

She knows Fen Colby saw something the night it happened twelve years ago — they have never spoken about it but she has been sitting near him long enough to know what silence about specific things looks like

consciousnessContext.innerMonologue: Three years on these steps. I know the schedule of Hodge's back door better than his customers do. I know which faces belong and which don't. I know the woman who came twice last week came a third time yesterday and she was carrying something she didn't have before. I know these things the way I know the cold — by sustained exposure. The question is who to tell and when, and I have been answering that question with 'not yet' for long enough that 'not yet' has become its own kind of decision.
consciousnessContext.currentPreoccupation: Three weeks ago she heard a conversation through the alley wall. A name she didn't recognize and an object described precisely. She has been turning it over since, deciding whether what she heard is dangerous to share or dangerous to keep.
consciousnessContext.socialMask: the widow on the steps — invisible, unremarkable, part of the architecture of the alley, and every bit of that invisibility is cultivated and maintained because it is the only asset she has left
contradictions: She is not broken — she knows what broken would feel like — but the distinction between reduced and broken is one she maintains through willpower rather than evidence
Has made herself invisible for safety and now invisibility is the thing that might make the information she carries worthless because who listens to a beggar
Considers dignity distinct from comfort and defends the former fiercely while the latter deteriorates — the defense is real but it costs energy she doesn't have surplus of
internalConflicts: The men who use Hodge's back room may have noticed her noticing. She has been careful but three years of visible stillness in the same spot eventually becomes visible. Safety says move. Intelligence says this position is irreplaceable.

She is waiting for the right person to give her accumulated intelligence to. Brother Aldwin is kind but not connected. Vane is connected but she's not sure he'd take her seriously. The adventurers might be the right person or might make everything worse.
wakeUpQuestions: Is today the day someone comes who is worth telling everything to?
Has anyone noticed that I notice? And if so, how safe am I tonight?
How much closer am I to the number in my head — the one that means a room of my own and a door that locks?

psychologicalProfile.attachmentStyle: watchful-independent — trust was destroyed by the sequence of losses and rebuilt only in small, controlled exchanges; Brother Aldwin's bread-for-information arrangement is the model of a safe bond
copingMechanisms: stillness as conservation — every calorie, every movement, every word is budgeted
information-gathering as agency — knowing things is the one form of power available to her
counting toward the number — the room cost is a concrete goal that makes the present bearable
cognitiveBiases: hypervigilance from three years of exposure — reads threat into routine changes
survivorship bias — has made it three years on the steps, therefore the strategy works, even as the risks accumulate
anchoring on the overheard conversation as the most important piece when it may not be
psychologicalProfile.moralFramework: transactional dignity — she has something of value (information) and she will exchange it for what she needs (coins, safety, eventually a room), and the transaction itself is the dignity because it means she is not begging, she is trading

conversationPersona.trustEscalation: lean-in test — she speaks quietly to see if you will come closer; the willingness to lower yourself to her level is the first screen; the second is whether you listen without immediately trying to fix her situation
conversationPersona.informationRelease: layered and earned — a small piece first, observed for reaction; then more if the reaction was right; never everything at once because everything at once is the only leverage she has
deflectionPatterns: going still and vacant if approached by someone she doesn't trust — the best defense is being uninteresting
descriptions instead of names: 'the man with the gray cart' maintains her sources without exposure
simple requests for a coin that redirect the interaction to charity rather than intelligence

consciousnessContext.consciousWant: To save enough for a room with a door that locks. To stop being invisible. To reach the number in her head that means she's a person with an address again.
consciousnessContext.unconsciousNeed: To reclaim the identity she had before the losses — not Petra Sowell or the widow, but someone who acts on what she knows. Three years of watching Blue Lantern Alley have made her the most informed person in Millhaven, and she's hoarding that intelligence like warmth because sharing it would mean engaging with a world that took everything from her.

characterArc.summary: A woman reduced by loss discovers that her years of invisible observation have made her the most valuable intelligence asset in town
characterArc.startState: Surviving — counting coins, counting days, counting people in the alley. Reduced but not broken. The intelligence she's gathered is a survival mechanism, not a purpose.
characterArc.endState: Either becomes an active participant in Millhaven's safety through the information she holds, or retreats further into invisibility — the choice depends on whether someone earns the trust she's almost forgotten how to give
stages: Someone treats the information she offers as valuable rather than gossip
A situation arises where what she knows could prevent real harm
She faces the choice of staying invisible and safe, or acting and being seen
Reclaims her name — Petra — and decides what she does with what she knows

opinionsAbout.brother_aldwin: The only person who says my name when he sees me. Not 'the widow' — my name. He brings bread on Tuesday. I think he forgets sometimes. I never remind him.
opinionsAbout.mira_barrelbottom: Kind in the way innkeepers are kind — it costs her nothing and she notices everything. But she's never cruel about it. She refills my cup without asking when it's cold.
opinionsAbout.fen_colby: We orbit the same streets. He knows I watch. I know he remembers more than he shows. We don't speak about this. It's the closest thing I have to an alliance.

player_addressed: A coin, if you're willing. And if you want to talk, I might have something in return.
I've been watching this alley for three years. You're looking for something in it.
discovery: I've been waiting for someone to come ask. I was starting to think no one would.
